title: Distributed Key-Value Store — System Design
description: Design a horizontally scalable, highly available key-value store in the Dynamo/Cassandra lineage. The themes are partitioning by consistent hashing, replication, tunable quorum consistency, and how the system heals divergence after failures.

nodes.label: Client / SDK
nodes.description: Application using the store; talks to any node.

nodes.label: Coordinator Node
nodes.description: The node that receives a request and coordinates replicas (any node can play this role).

nodes.label: Partitioner (Hash Ring)
nodes.description: Maps a key to its preference list of replica nodes via consistent hashing.

nodes.label: Replica Node A
nodes.description: Owns a range of the keyspace; stores and replicates data.

nodes.label: Replica Node B
nodes.description: Next replica on the ring for the key's preference list.

nodes.label: Replica Node C
nodes.description: Third replica on the ring for the key's preference list.

nodes.label: Local Storage Engine
nodes.description: Per-node on-disk store (e.g. LSM tree) holding this node's key ranges.

nodes.label: Membership / Gossip
nodes.description: Tracks which nodes are alive and the ring topology via gossip.

nodes.label: Anti-Entropy Service
nodes.description: Background repair of divergent replicas via Merkle-tree comparison.

nodes.label: Hinted-Handoff Store
nodes.description: Temporarily holds writes destined for a down replica, replayed on recovery.

functional: put(key, value) and get(key) over an opaque byte value.
The store scales horizontally: adding nodes increases capacity and throughput.
Survive node and whole-zone failures without losing accepted writes.
nonFunctional: Always available for writes — prefer accepting a write over rejecting it (AP leaning).
Tunable consistency: callers choose how many replicas must respond per operation.
Low, predictable latency (single-digit ms) at the storage layer.
No single point of failure; no special master node.
Incremental scalability — adding/removing a node moves minimal data.

capacity.label: Op latency target
capacity.note: Local storage engine + parallel replica calls.

api.description: Write a value. The coordinator replicates to N nodes and waits for W acks.
Read a value. The coordinator queries N nodes and returns once R agree (or all versions on conflict).
Delete a key. Implemented as a write of a tombstone so the delete propagates and survives repair.

dataModel.name: key-value record (per node)
dataModel.note: What each replica stores locally for a key it owns.

dataModel.name: ring / token map
dataModel.note: Control-plane state: which token ranges each node owns.

fields.name: node_id
fields.type: owner of the range ending at token

patterns.name: Consistent hashing
patterns.what: Map keys to nodes on a hash ring so adding/removing a node moves only a fraction of keys.
patterns.whenToUse: Any horizontally-partitioned store that must scale incrementally.
patterns.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

patterns.name: Virtual nodes
patterns.what: Give each physical node many ring positions to even out load and speed rebalancing.
patterns.whenToUse: When physical nodes are few or heterogeneous and naive hashing causes hotspots.
patterns.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

patterns.name: Quorum consistency (N/W/R)
patterns.what: Require W write acks and R read responses; W+R>N gives strong-ish reads.
patterns.whenToUse: When you want a per-operation knob between availability and consistency.
patterns.group: Replicate Each Key to N Nodes

patterns.name: Vector clocks
patterns.what: Per-key causal version vectors to detect concurrent (conflicting) writes.
patterns.whenToUse: Multi-writer systems where last-write-wins would silently drop data.

patterns.name: Hinted handoff
patterns.what: Store a write meant for a down replica elsewhere and replay it on recovery.
patterns.whenToUse: To keep accepting writes (preserve W) during transient node failure.

patterns.name: Merkle-tree anti-entropy
patterns.what: Compare replica data via hash trees and repair only the differing ranges.
patterns.whenToUse: Background reconciliation of replicas that diverged during failures.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Single-Node Key-Value Store (the baseline)
description: Start with the simplest thing that works: one node running a local storage engine. A client sends get/put to that node, which reads and writes the single on-disk store. For a small dataset this is correct, fast, and trivial to reason about.

It fails on two of the stated requirements at once. Capacity: one machine's disk and memory cap how many keys you can hold, so billions of keys don't fit. Availability: that node is a single point of failure — if it dies or its disk is lost, every key is unavailable and accepted writes are gone.

So the baseline motivates the whole design. To hold more than one machine's worth of data you must spread keys across nodes (partitioning); to survive a node loss you must keep more than one copy (replication). Everything that follows removes a specific pain from here.
steps.decisionPrompt: One node holds every key on its local disk. What two requirements break first as data and traffic grow?
whyNow: Establishing the single-node baseline makes it obvious why we partition (capacity) and replicate (availability) — the next two steps each fix one failure of this design.

recap.after: Keys stored and served from one node's local engine.
recap.newRisk: One machine caps capacity, and a single node loss takes all data — must partition and replicate.

traps.trap: Shipping a single beefy node ('just use a bigger box') as the answer.
traps.why: It caps at one machine's disk/RAM and is a single point of failure — a node loss loses every accepted write.
traps.instead: Partition keys across many nodes for capacity and replicate each key for survival.

steps.title: 2. Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing
description: Place nodes on a hash ring; a key hashes to a position and is owned by the next node clockwise. Adding or removing a node only reshuffles the keys between it and its neighbor, not the whole keyspace.
Give each physical node many virtual nodes (tokens) so load is even and a departing node's range is absorbed by many peers rather than one.
Any node can receive a request and act as coordinator; it uses the ring to find the key's owners.
steps.decisionPrompt: With plain modulo hashing, what fraction of keys move when you add the Nth node? With consistent hashing?

concepts.definition: Hash both keys and nodes onto a ring; a key belongs to the next node clockwise.
concepts.whyItMatters: Adding/removing a node moves ~1/N of keys instead of nearly all of them.
concepts.example: 10 nodes -> add one -> only ~9% of keys relocate.
concepts.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

concepts.definition: Each physical node claims many ring positions.
concepts.whyItMatters: Smooths load imbalance and spreads a failed node's range across many peers.
concepts.example: 256 vnodes per physical node is common.
concepts.group: Partition the Keyspace with Consistent Hashing

whyNow: Partitioning is the foundation — every later decision (replication, quorum, repair) is defined relative to a key's position on the ring.

recap.after: Keys spread across many nodes; incremental scaling.
recap.newRisk: A single copy per key — a node loss loses data; need replication next.

traps.why: Changing node count remaps almost every key, causing a storm of data movement.
traps.instead: Hash onto a ring with virtual nodes so only neighbors' ranges shift.

steps.title: 3. Replicate Each Key to N Nodes
description: Store each key on the next N distinct nodes clockwise from its position — its preference list. N=3 is typical, spread across failure domains (zones).
The coordinator forwards every write to all N replicas and every read to all N (or enough of them).
Replication is what makes the store durable and available: any replica can serve the key.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you choose the N replicas so a single zone outage can't take all copies of a key?
whyNow: Durability and availability both come from replication; it sets up the consistency knob in the next step.

recap.newRisk: Replicas can disagree; need rules for how many must respond.

flows.name: Write replicated to N nodes
flows.note: The coordinator forwards a write to every node on the key's preference list so each holds a copy.

steps.title: 4. Tunable Consistency with Quorums
description: Let callers pick W (write acks required) and R (read responses required). If W + R > N, any read overlaps at least one node that saw the latest write — read-your-writes without a master.
Common defaults: N=3, W=2, R=2. Want faster writes? W=1. Want stronger reads? R=3. The knob is per operation.
The coordinator returns success as soon as the quorum is met, not after all N — that's what keeps latency low and availability high.
steps.decisionPrompt: With N=3, which (W,R) pairs give strong reads, and which favor write availability?

concepts.definition: Requiring overlapping read and write sets so a read sees the latest committed write.
concepts.whyItMatters: Gives a consistency dial without a single leader.
concepts.example: N=3, W=2, R=2: write set and read set share at least one node.

whyNow: This is the heart of a Dynamo-style store — articulating the W+R>N tradeoff is what the interviewer is listening for.

recap.after: Per-op consistency dial via W and R.
recap.newRisk: Below-quorum or concurrent writes create divergent versions to reconcile.

traps.why: Concurrent writes and sloppy quorums (hinted handoff) can still produce conflicting versions.
traps.instead: Say W+R>N gives strong-ish read-your-writes, and pair it with version reconciliation.

steps.title: 4a. Choosing N, W, and R
description: The quorum knobs are a small decision with outsized effect. With N replicas, requiring W write acks and R read responses, the rule W + R > N guarantees any read set overlaps any write set — so a read sees the latest committed write (read-your-writes) without a leader.

Tune for the workload: N=3, W=2, R=2 is the balanced default. Want faster, more-available writes? W=1 (but reads may miss a fresh write unless R=3). Want the strongest reads? R=3. The point is these are per-operation dials, not a global setting.

Sloppy quorums (writing to whatever N nodes are reachable, with hinted handoff) trade some consistency for availability during failures — useful, but it means W+R>N no longer strictly guarantees overlap, so reconcile with versioning.
steps.decisionPrompt: With N=3, which (W,R) pairs give read-your-writes, and which favor write availability?

concepts.definition: Choosing write/read quorum sizes whose sets always overlap, giving strong-ish reads without a leader.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the precise lever between consistency and availability, tunable per operation.
concepts.example: N=3,W=2,R=2 overlaps; W=1,R=1 is fast but may read stale.

traps.trap: Claiming a quorum gives full linearizability.
traps.why: Concurrent writes and sloppy quorums still produce divergent versions to reconcile.
traps.instead: Say W+R>N gives read-your-writes, and pair it with version reconciliation.

recap.after: N/W/R chosen per workload with the W+R>N rule understood.
recap.newRisk: Sloppy quorums and concurrent writes still need version reconciliation (next step).

steps.title: 5. Detect and Reconcile Conflicting Writes
description: When two clients write the same key concurrently (or during a partition), replicas hold different versions. The store must not silently drop one.

Attach a vector clock to each value. On read, if versions are causally ordered, return the newest; if they're concurrent, return both as siblings for the application (or a registered merge function) to reconcile.
Last-write-wins (by timestamp) is the simpler alternative, but it can lose writes under clock skew — call out the tradeoff.
steps.decisionPrompt: Two clients update a shopping cart concurrently on different replicas. How does the store avoid losing an item?

concepts.definition: A per-key map of nodeId -> counter capturing causal history of a value.
concepts.whyItMatters: Distinguishes 'this version supersedes that' from 'these happened concurrently'.
concepts.example: Cart versions {A:2} and {A:1,B:1} are concurrent -> merge as siblings.

whyNow: A store that accepts writes during partitions must have an answer for divergence; vector clocks are the principled one.

recap.before: Versions assumed to be totally ordered.
recap.after: Concurrent versions detected and surfaced for merge.
recap.newRisk: Application must handle siblings; vector clocks can grow and need pruning.

traps.trap: Defaulting to last-write-wins without mentioning the risk.
traps.why: Clock skew makes a stale write 'win', silently destroying a newer value.
traps.instead: Use vector clocks (or LWW only where loss is acceptable, stated explicitly).

steps.title: 6. Tolerate Failures: Hinted Handoff & Anti-Entropy
description: If a replica is temporarily down, the coordinator still needs W acks. With hinted handoff it writes to a substitute node tagged with a hint; when the intended replica recovers, the hint is replayed to it.

Replicas still drift over time (dropped hints, longer outages). A background anti-entropy process compares replicas using Merkle trees and repairs only the key ranges whose hashes differ — cheap reconciliation.
Read repair complements this: when a read sees stale replicas, the coordinator writes the latest version back to them inline.
steps.decisionPrompt: A replica is down for an hour. How do you keep accepting writes, and how is it brought back in sync?

concepts.definition: Temporarily storing a write for a down replica on another node, replayed on recovery.
concepts.whyItMatters: Preserves write availability (W) during transient failures.
concepts.example: Node B down -> write goes to D with hint 'for B' -> replayed when B returns.

concepts.definition: Hash-tree comparison of replica data to find and repair only divergent ranges.
concepts.example: Top hashes match -> in sync; differ -> descend to find the divergent range.

whyNow: Failure handling is where availability is actually earned; naming hinted handoff + Merkle anti-entropy shows real distributed-systems depth.

recap.before: A down replica blocks quorum or causes data loss.
recap.after: Writes still succeed; replicas re-converge in the background.
recap.newRisk: Hints can pile up; anti-entropy and read-repair cost background bandwidth.

failureDrills.scenario: A node is down during many writes, then rejoins.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Hinted writes are replayed; remaining drift is closed by anti-entropy/read-repair.
failureDrills.mitigation: Hinted handoff + Merkle-tree repair + read repair together.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Both sides keep accepting writes (AP); on heal, versions reconcile via vector clocks.

flows.name: Hinted handoff while a replica is down
flows.note: A down replica can't ack, so the coordinator stores a hint on a substitute and replays it on recovery — the write still reaches W.

steps.title: 7. Membership, Gossip, and Rebalancing
description: There is no master. Nodes learn who is alive and who owns what via a gossip protocol — each node periodically exchanges state with a few peers; topology and liveness converge.
When a node joins or leaves, it claims/releases token ranges and streams the affected key ranges to/from neighbors. Virtual nodes keep this movement small and parallel.
Clients/coordinators route using their (eventually consistent) view of the ring; a stale view just means an extra hop, not incorrect data.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why prefer gossip-based membership over a central coordinator service tracking liveness?

concepts.definition: Nodes periodically exchange state with random peers; information spreads epidemically.
concepts.whyItMatters: Decentralized failure detection and topology with no single point of failure.
concepts.example: Each node gossips with 3 peers per second; the cluster converges in O(log N) rounds.

whyNow: Closing on decentralized membership ties the design back to the 'no SPOF / incremental scalability' non-functional requirements.

recap.after: Self-managing cluster that scales and heals without a master.
recap.newRisk: Eventual-consistency of the ring view; flapping nodes; rebalancing bandwidth.

bottlenecks.issue: Rebalancing floods the network when a big node joins.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Throttle streaming; virtual nodes spread the transfer across many peers.
Phi-accrual failure detector with hysteresis before declaring down.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Dynamo-Style KV Store
finalDesign.description: A masterless ring of nodes (with virtual nodes) partitions keys by consistent hashing and replicates each to N nodes across zones. Any node coordinates an op, using tunable W/R quorums for the consistency/availability dial. Concurrent writes are reconciled with vector clocks; transient failures use hinted handoff, and replicas re-converge via Merkle-tree anti-entropy and read repair. Membership and topology spread by gossip.

functional.how: Coordinator routes to the preference list; delete is a tombstone write.
Consistent hashing + virtual nodes move minimal data when nodes are added.
N replicas across zones; hinted handoff and anti-entropy heal drift.

nonFunctional.how: Sloppy quorum + hinted handoff keep accepting writes during failures.
Per-op W and R with the W+R>N rule.
Masterless ring; gossip membership; any node coordinates.
Virtual nodes spread token ranges so joins/leaves move little data.

say: Confirm AP leaning: prefer availability/partition tolerance over strict consistency.
Establish it must be masterless and incrementally scalable.
Agree on tunable consistency as a goal, not one fixed level.
Partition with consistent hashing + virtual nodes.
Replicate to N nodes across zones.
Introduce W/R quorums and the W+R>N rule.
Handle concurrent writes with vector clocks and siblings.
Cover failures: hinted handoff, Merkle anti-entropy, read repair.
Map each requirement to a mechanism.
State tradeoffs: eventual consistency, sibling handling, repair bandwidth.
Note when you'd instead pick a CP store (need linearizability).

expectations: Partitions with consistent hashing and replicates to N nodes.
Reasons about sloppy quorums, partition behavior, and CAP positioning.
Discusses failure detection (phi-accrual), rebalancing cost, and when a CP design is the right call instead.

followUps: When would you choose a CP store (e.g. Raft-based) over this AP design?
How do vector clocks grow unbounded, and how do you prune them safely?
How would you add range scans or secondary indexes to a hash-partitioned store?
How does a sloppy quorum differ from a strict quorum, and what does it cost in consistency?
How would you back up and restore a single token range without downtime?
